fields.name: 18. How to create a Business Plan
fields.content-01:
0–5 min – Introduction

‍

  • Explain what a business plan is and why it matters in entrepreneurship.
  • Emphasize how a plan helps turn an idea into a realistic project.

5–15 min – Mini Lecture

‍
Step 1: Identify the Problem/Need
 What problem exists on the market, or what need do we want to respond to?

Step 2: Define the Solution (Product/Service)
What product or service do we offer and how does it solve the problem?

Step 3: Build the Plan – three key elements:

  • Target group (Customers): Who is the product/service for?
  • Resources: What is needed to start (people, space, materials, knowledge)?
  • Costs & Revenue: What are the expenses and how will we make money?

Example:
‍

Problem: Students often don’t have access to healthy and affordable snacks near school.
Solution: Open a small school snack bar with healthy sandwiches and smoothies.
Plan:

  • Target group: students and teachers.
  • Resources: 2 student sellers, small school space, ingredients.
  • Costs & Revenue: initial cost €200, revenue from sales €1–2 per snack.

15–35 min – Group Exercise

‍

  • Divide students into groups of 3–4.
  • Each group gets a worksheet and task to:
    1. Choose a problem/need (or receive a predefined idea, e.g., hair salon, IT support, eco-product).
    2. Define the product/service.
    3. Develop a plan: target group, resources, costs and revenues.
  • Optional: each group presents their plan briefly (1–2 minutes).

‍

35–45 min – Discussion and Wrap-up

‍

  • Reflective questions:
    • What was hardest about developing your plan?
    • Did you agree on the target group and resources?
    • Was the plan realistic and achievable?

‍
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

By the end of this lesson, students will be able to:

  • Understand what a business plan is and why it is important.
  • Identify a problem or market need that their business plan addresses.
  • Develop the key elements of a business plan (product/service, target group, resources, costs and revenues).
  • Present and defend their business plan in front of others.
fields.content-03:
Learning outcomes

‍

Students will:

  • Analyze a business idea and define a problem/need in the market.
  • Identify a target customer group and the resources required to implement the idea.
  • Develop a basic business plan using key elements.
  • Practice presenting and arguing for their plan.
fields.content-04:
Materials and tools

‍

  • Whiteboard or flipchart
  • Worksheet with guiding questions (or student notebooks)
  • Examples of 2–3 business plans (short versions)
  • Markers, sticky notes
fields.content-05:
Assessment

‍

  • Informal: observation during group work.
  • Optional: short written reflection (exit ticket):
    “What is the most important element of a business plan and why?”
  • Evaluation of clarity and logic during presentations.

‍

Tips and tricks

‍

  • Use examples from students’ vocations (hospitality, IT, economics, tourism, etc.).
  • Visualize the plan as a simple chart (Problem → Solution → Target group → Resources → Costs/Revenue).
  • Encourage practical thinking: “Could this actually be implemented?”

Celebrate creativity but stress the importance of feasibility.
